reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: Substrate (dipolarophile) dissolved in DES; kept separate from nitroacetate until mixing point to control stoichiometry

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: 1,3-Dipole precursor dissolved in DES; 2.0 equiv needed for full conversion as nitrile oxide can dimerize

reagents.name: phenylacetylene
reagents.notes: Terminal alkyne; regioselective cycloaddition gives 5-phenylisoxazole regioisomer

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates ethoxycarbonyl nitrile oxide in situ at 120°C; excess used to compensate for dimerization

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium and may promote nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; lowers melting point of TBAB

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (EtO2C-CNO) in situ. The nitro group undergoes elimination of water facilitated by the DES medium at 120°C.
Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide (1,3-dipole) with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile). Regioselective addition places phenyl at C-5 of the isoxazole ring.
Competing side reaction: dimerization of nitrile oxide to furoxan if not trapped rapidly by the dipolarophile. This explains the need for 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate.

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; stable at room temperature; pumped separately to control stoichiometry at T-mixer
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; must not be preheated alone to avoid premature nitrile oxide generation and dimerization before mixing with dipolarophile

mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate should be mixed at a T-mixer immediately before entering the heated coil reactor. Premixing at room temperature is acceptable since nitrile oxide generation requires 120°C. Both streams in DES are combined and then enter the heated zone simultaneously, ensuring the nitrile oxide is generated in the presence of the dipolarophile to minimize dimerization.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species; reaction proceeds under air in batch protocol.
quench_reasoning: No quench needed inline; product is stable. Batch workup (water quench, DCM extraction) is for isolation only.
wavelength_reasoning: Not a photochemical reaction; purely thermal activation at 120°C.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate at high temperature is well-established. The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide formation from ethyl nitroacetate (whether via nitronate anion or direct thermal elimination) in DES is debated — could involve DES-mediated base-assisted dehydration. DES viscosity (~50–100 cP at RT, much lower at 120°C ~5–10 cP) may require back-pressure regulation and wider tubing if pumping issues arise. TBAB/EG DES is compatible with standard PTFE/PFA tubing. The 2.0 equiv of nitroacetate suggests significant nitrile oxide dimerization in batch; flow may reduce this.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides: (1) Superior heat transfer — DES is viscous (high η), and narrow-bore tubing (0.75–1.0 mm ID) gives surface-to-volume ratio ~4000 m⁻¹ vs ~50 m⁻¹ in batch vial, enabling rapid heating to 120°C and precise temperature control. (2) Reduced nitrile oxide dimerization — rapid, efficient mixing at T-mixer ensures nitrile oxide is generated in intimate contact with phenylacetylene. (3) Safety — superheated DES at 120°C in small volume reduces thermal hazard. (4) Potential to reduce ethyl nitroacetate excess from 2.0 to ~1.5 equiv due to better mixing, improving atom economy. (5) Short residence time (15 min) is ideal for continuous processing with high throughput.
